---
title: "FW 536 — Day 3 morning lab (mixed models) — Your Name"
author: "Your Name Here"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Day 3 morning lab — Mixed models problem set

This template matches the morning (Part 1) problems in
[`problem_set.html`](problem_set.html#morning). Read each problem statement there,
write your work below, **Knit** to HTML, and upload the `.html` to the matching
Canvas assignment.

For setup help see the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html).

---

# Setup

```{r}
# Load packages you'll need. Add or remove as appropriate.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(lme4)
library(nlme)
library(AICcmodavg)
library(MuMIn)
```

```{r data}
# Real-data problems read these files from the data/ folder.
# Problem 1 — streams (stream_density.csv): 100 streams, three observations each
Streams <- read.csv("data/stream_density.csv")
Streams$Stream <- factor(Streams$Stream)

# Problem 2 — length-weight (length_weight.csv)
LenW <- read.csv("data/length_weight.csv")
LenW$Subject <- as.factor(LenW$Subject)

# Problem 3 — von Bertalanffy growth (fish_growth.csv)
AgeLen <- groupedData(Length ~ Age | Subject,
                      data = read.csv("data/fish_growth.csv"))

# Problem 4 — trophic position (qcbs_w6_data.csv)
dat <- read.csv("data/qcbs_w6_data.csv")
dat$Fish_Species <- as.factor(dat$Fish_Species)
dat$Lake         <- as.factor(dat$Lake)
dat$Z_Length     <- scale(dat$Fish_Length)[, 1]
dat$Z_TP         <- scale(dat$Trophic_Pos)[, 1]
```

---

# Problem 1 — Streams: complete / no / partial pooling (stream_density.csv)

> Fit `lm(Density ~ 1)`, `lm(Density ~ Stream - 1)`, and
> `lme(Density ~ 1, random = ~ 1 | Stream)`. Compare pooling behaviour, residual
> structure, shrinkage of the most extreme stream, and the ICC.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (Streams data frame is loaded above)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 2 — Length–weight random intercepts (length_weight.csv)

> Fit `lme(Weight ~ Length, random = ~ 1 | Subject)` (and the `lmer` equivalent).
> Report the fixed slope, the among-subject intercept SD, the residual SD and ICC,
> then test a random slope with `anova(..., refit = FALSE)`.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (LenW data frame is loaded above)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 3 — Von Bertalanffy nonlinear mixed model (fish_growth.csv)

> Fit the fixed-effect `nls` von Bertalanffy curve and the `nlme` version with a
> random $L_\infty$ per fish. Compare residual structure and residual SE, and
> interpret the per-fish BLUPs.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (AgeLen groupedData is loaded above)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 4 — Trophic position: crossed random effects (qcbs_w6_data.csv)

> Fit the candidate `lmer` models (M1, M3, M4, M7, M8), rank with `aictab()`, and
> read the variance components of the best model. Discuss the few-levels risk.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (dat data frame is loaded above)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 5 — Detection probability: binomial GLMM

> Given the `VarCorr` output in the problem, interpret the covariate slope and
> compare the two random-effect SDs, compute the detection probability at mean
> conditions, and translate the covariate slope into an odds ratio.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (this problem is conceptual; show your arithmetic in code)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 6 — Nested random effects + overdispersion

> Diagram the nesting, write the Poisson GLMM, distinguish `(1 | unit/transect)`
> from `(1 | unit) + (1 | transect)`, and give the Pearson-dispersion check plus
> two fixes for overdispersion.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here (conceptual; include the dispersion-check expression)
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# (Optional) Embed a photo of hand-written work

For problems easier to do on paper, photograph and embed:

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.cap="Hand-derivation for Problem N"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/problemN_derivation.jpg")
```

(Change `eval=FALSE` to `eval=TRUE` after saving an image. See the
[R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html) for details.)

---

# Submission checklist

- [ ] Answered every morning (Part 1) problem from `problem_set.html`.
- [ ] Loaded the data files (`stream_density.csv`, `length_weight.csv`, `fish_growth.csv`, `qcbs_w6_data.csv`) from `data/`.
- [ ] Knitted to HTML without errors.
- [ ] Photos (if any) embedded with `self_contained: true` in the YAML.
- [ ] Uploaded the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.
